description: A complaint is presented but no date is given for the complaint.
Minutes of January 9 Called Meeting referenced, but not submitted for review.
responses: Presbytery agrees with this exception and will submit the
minutes for review in 2019.

description: Censures of deposition and excommunication not correctly administered; no record of required language.
responses: Presbytery agrees with the exception and will amend the
minutes to the correct language and resubmit that part of SM 53
minutes for review.

description: TEs not assigned to the membership of particular churches.
Presbytery dissolved pastoral relation with no explanation.
Presbytery omitted examination in church government for licensure candidate without explanation.
Presbytery adopted a statement asserting its right to “declare certain exceptions unteachable.” Presbytery may rule any stated difference unacceptable but may not “make laws to bind the conscience.”
The presbytery erred by revisiting a theological examination after it was sustained and arrested with a motion to reconsider.
responses: Calvary Presbytery agrees with the RPR that, regrettably,
we erred by revisiting a theological examination after it was
sustained and arrested without a motion to reconsider. In response,
at our July 27, 2018 meeting, we adopted the following motion to
provide clarity going forward: “Presbytery must deal with the
‘handling of exceptions’ within a man’s examination on the same
day and on the floor of presbytery during his formal presbytery
examination unless a motion to reconsider is properly received and
taken after the fact.” In addition, we acknowledge that the restricting
of TE’s teaching of his exception on LC 109 was procedurally out
of order and no longer consider it in effect.

description: Stated differences not judged with the prescribed categories.
responses: Regrettably, Calvary Presbytery failed to identify
the nature of the exception granted to transfer candidate [name
omitted] on LC 109. Though his exception was properly recorded
(see Appendix 2-G), it should have been approved with the
following prescription: “more than semantic, but ‘not out of accord
with any fundamental of our system of doctrine,’’’ per RAO 16-3.e5. This was the intent of the court in allowing the exception.
While the Presbytery reported the difference,
they did not judge the stated difference according to the prescribed
categories.
On October 25, 2018, Calvary Presbytery voted
to grant TE’s [name omitted] exception on LC 109 as “more than
semantic, but ‘not out of accord with any fundamental of our system
of doctrine,’” per RAO 16-3.3-5. Regrettably, this action should
have taken place on Oct. 22, 2015 when he first filed his difference
with the presbytery. We trust that this action satisfies the concerns
of the RPR.

description: No record of call to a definite work.
Presbytery went into Executive Session, but no minutes recorded.
responses: Canada West acknowledges the error and apologizes for
the oversight. The minutes of the Executive Session are attached
with this response, having been approved at our Stated October
Presbytery Meeting in 2018.

description: no record of an examination of original languages or seminary degree accepted in lieu of such an examination of a candidate from another denomination (OPC).

responses: The Presbytery agrees with the exceptions as recorded

He was an Assistant Pastor so a congregational meeting was not

The Presbytery approved a correction to the records at the
November 13, 2018 stated meeting of Presbytery.

description: No record of examination in Church History (i.e. no record of examination in all areas).
responses: The Presbytery agrees with the exceptions and apologizes
for not entering information into the records. The Presbytery assures
that the examinations were done correctly but there was a failure in
recording the action in the minutes. Hopefully our seriousness in
correcting these exceptions in the 2018 minutes will show adherence

description: TE laboring out of bounds, without concurrence of Presbytery in whose bounds he labors.
responses: The Presbytery agrees with the exception. The Presbytery
permitted along term te who was without a call to accept a job
offer in another Presbytery who wanted to remain in the Central
Florida Presbytery to see how things would work out for him. At the
November 13, 2018 meeting of Presbytery, the Presbytery removed
its permission for him to labor out of bounds.

description: No record of review of session records.
responses: The Presbytery agrees with the exception. In 2018 all
session records but two were reviewed. A workshop was held in

description: TE was divested but was not assigned to a church.
responses: The Presbytery agrees with the exception. The TE
involved was without call for several years and eventually chose
another occupation. He remained in the church he had attended for
several years.
Still not clear if he has been assigned membership
in a local congregation.

description: No record of congregational meeting to dissolve pastoral relationship. Response [2019] – This concerns TE [name omitted]. The Chairman of Presbytery’s committee “Minister and His Work” reported he was very involved with the situation at the church and he has assured Presbytery that the congregation approved his dissolution.
responses: There needs to be a congregational meeting record.

description: Presbytery Minutes record receipt of commission reports; but the minutes of commissions were not entered in Presbytery Minutes for installation of TE [name omitted].
responses: The CFP agrees with these exceptions of
substance. It recognizes the failure to send or receive commission
reports and including in the official records. Many hours have been
spent reconstructing, finding and interviewing various people to
locate or affirm the commissions. The Presbytery knows it is an
important part of the record to have commission reports and is
correcting the situation.
Unclear how Presbytery is correcting the problem
of not entering commission minutes into Presbytery minutes given
no evidence of correction of these omissions in subsequent minutes.
The Central Florida Presbytery agrees with these
exceptions.
Rationale: [2019]: Presbytery has not shown how the problem of
not entering the record of actions by a commission into Presbytery
minutes is being corrected.

description: This commission involves the installation of [name omitted]. A commission was elected at the April 22, 2014 Presbytery meeting and the TE was installed on April 27, 2014. responses: Unclear which date [name omitted]’s commission

responses: Unclear which date [name omitted]’s commission

description: This exception involves two TEs [names omitted]. The minutes mistakenly entered another TE’s name and the minutes have been corrected. responses: November 13, 2018 minutes do not include the

description: No record of the steps required for a case without process per BCO 38-1 have been followed. No full statement of facts, no statement by individual of intent to confess and permit the court to render the judgment without process.
Session record review not included.
responses: The report of the Sessional Records Committee at the
January meeting was preliminary, in that churches have until the
September meeting to have their records reviewed and approved. As
such, we recorded only that the Committee Chairman gave a report,
without providing details of his report.
In the future, CGP agrees to record the narrative of the
Committee report at every meeting in which a report is given.
With respect to the January 21, 2017 Stated Presbytery meeting,
CGP minutes will be amended to include the following report which
was given orally by the Chairman of the Sessional Records
Committee.
1. Prayer
2. Overall submission of the 2016 minutes was better and
much more timely than last year. Eleven churches
emailed their minutes in and seven were brought in for
review today. All minutes indicate nothing was done
contrary to good order.
3. Clerks need to be more careful to document 1) Reports
from Presbytery meetings, 2) Deacons Minutes review.
3) Annual Statistical Reports and 4) A listing of
baptisms, marriages, deaths (recommend you put these
items in the minutes of your last meeting of the year).
4. Some facts. CGP churches held 103 Stated Meetings,
27 Called Meetings and 8 Congregational Meetings.
5. The best minutes were from [church names omitted].
6. Prayer.
At the CGP Stated Presbytery meeting on September 11, 2018, a
motion was moved, seconded and passed to approve the above
response to RPR. This response will be included in the minutes of
the September 11 [th] meeting.

responses: Line 41 was a typing error, in which the clerk inadvertently

responses: We humbly apologize for our failure to comply with the

Presbytery or a committee of the Presbytery.

( BCO 21-4.e; 40-2; RAO 16-3.e.5.c; and WCF 8-2) – The following sentence in a man’s licensure exam appears to demonstrate a faulty Christology that strikes at the vitals of religion : “... since christ became a man and has a physical body, and since we worship him for his divinity, not his humanity, I believe that images of the incarnate Christ used for the purposes of art and education are permissible and do not violate the second commandment” (emphasis added). This was in his statement of difference with WLC 109, but the issue being cited involves Christology and not images, per se.
( BCO 21-4 and RAO 16-3.e.5) – No record of PCA history exam.
Candidate examined for ordination at September 15, 2018 meeting and commission minutes indicate that candidate was installed without ordination.
Use of extraordinary clause not explained. pres byte ry received a letter from a church asking for extraordinary clause and stating rationale, but no rationale was adopted by the Presbytery.
No record that minister transferring into presbytery was examined for Christian Experience and views.
Three examined candidates gave identical, verbatim stated differences. Our rules require that candidates express their stated differences “in their own words.”
responses: Gulfstream acknowledges this mistake. The minutes of
January 17, 2017 have been amended to include the following
statements:
Item 9.b. TE [name omitted], [church name omitted], transfer exam
from Presbytery of Central Florida [PCA]
- When asked about changes in previous view of Confession,
Catechisms, or Book of Church Order, TE [name omitted]
replied,
o [In reference to WCF, Ch. XXI, Paragraph 8, & WLC
117, & WSC 60] “I do not believe that Scripture
excludes all forms of recreation on the Sabbath Day.
Recreation, especially when understood in the context
of the fellowship of God’s people, and not taken to
extremes, can be a means to enjoy both rest and
fellowship. (Gen. 2:2-3; Ex. 16:30; 31:15-17; Mark
2:27). I understand that this exception is more than
semantic, but not out of accord with any fundamental
of our system of doctrines
o [In reference to WLC 109] “Regarding sins forbidden
in the second commandment and specifically the
‘making of any representation of God, of all or of any
of the three persons, either inwardly in our mind, or
outwardly in any kind of image or likeness of any
creature whatsoever;’: I believe that images of Christ,
either in one’s mind or used for pedagogical purposes,
do not in all cases violate the second commandment.
Scripture itself gives narratives of Jesus. It is difficult,
if not impossible, to avoid forming mental pictures of
Jesus regarding his humanity while reading those
narratives. I understand that this exception is more than
semantic, but not out of accord with any fundamental
of our system of doctrines.”
Item 9.c. TE [name omitted] [church name omitted], transfer exam
from Presbytery of Central Florida [PCA]
- When asked about changes in previous view of Confession,
Catechisms, or Book of Church Order, TE [name omitted]
replied:
o [In reference to WCF, Ch. XXI. Paragraph 8 (likewise
WLC 117 and 119 and WSC 60) ] “ I do not agree that
the necessary implication of Scripture teaches the
unlawfulness of all forms of recreation on the Sabbath.
The Sabbath was initiated by God and also instituted
by him (Gen 2:2) primarily for the purpose of rest from
labor (Ex 20:9-11, 31:15-17). To the extent that such
recreations are indeed restful and moreover not sinful
in themselves, I find their exercise fully permissible on
the Sabbath. I believe this exception to be more than
semantic, but not out of accord with any fundamentals
of our system of doctrines.”
o [In reference to WLC 109] “The prohibition against
‘making any representation of God … inwardly in our
mind’ seems to me to be not only unreasonable but also
contrary to the pattern of Scripture, which itself
describes visible manifestations of God (Luke 3:22 and
Rev 1:13-16, for example). I believe the second
commandment would prohibit the making of any
mental images of our own design apart from Scripture,
however we do not sin in creating mental representations
of that which Scripture describes to us, so long as such
images are not objects of our worship. I believe this
exception to be more than semantic, but not out of
accord with any fundamentals of our system of
doctrines.”
Item 9.d. Rev. [name omitted], [church name omitted] transfer exam
from Evangelical Presbyterian Church [EPC]
- When asked about differences with the Confession, Catechisms,
or Book of Church Order, Rev. [name omitted] replied,
o [In reference to WCF 21.8, WLC 117, WSC 60] “ I do
not believe the Word of God demands we lay down all
“works, words, and thoughts” on the Lord’s Day.
These can be given to the Lord and done unto His
glory. I also believe that recreation can be done unto
the glory of God on the Sabbath and can in fact produce
rest that leads to the worship of God and
wholeheartedness.
o [In reference to WLC 109] “I believe the second
commandment prohibits us from creating mental
images of our own making. However, when Scripture
leads us to form mental images directly from the
descriptions of scripture, we do not sin or fall into
error. I believe this exception to be more than
semantic, but not out of accord with any fundamentals
of our system of doctrines.”
Said minutes were amended by action of Gulfstream Presbytery on
Please note further that Gulfstream, during its January 17, 2017
Stated Meeting, gave full consideration of the stated differences,
with further questioning and amplification. In each instance,
Gulfstream Presbytery judged the stated difference to be “to be more
than semantic, but ‘not out of accord with any fundamental of our
system of doctrine’” [ BCO 21-4, RAO 16-3.e.5)c)]
Copies of the amended minutes are available for inspection
upon request.

responses: Commissions are mentioned in the minutes. We include

description: Incomplete record of transfer examination: minutes do not reflect that TE [name omitted] was examined in his Christian experience; also he apparently was examined on Bible rather than his view on sacraments.
responses: Heritage Presbytery has reviewed the exception of
substance to our 2017 minutes and concluded that we indeed erred
in our transfer examination of TE [name omitted] who should have
been examined in re his views on The Sacraments. We have
reviewed his views accordingly and find them in accord with the
Standards. Thank you for pointing out this exception; we will
attempt to be more diligent in the future.
TE still needs to be examined on his Christian
experience.

responses: Review of Presbytery Records is correct in noting this
exception of substance. Houston Metro Presbytery will be more
diligent to include commission notes in future minutes.
The record of the commission’s actions needs to be
recorded in the Presbytery’s minutes.

description: No record of concurrence from home presbytery for TE examined for licensure.
responses: To begin, this was an unorthodox situation. TE
[name omitted] was a TE in missouri Presbytery, neighboring Iliana
to the West, serving in a revitalization work when their former pastor
exited quickly. TE [name omitted] expressed to the congregation
that he did not intend to move his credentials to Illiana, however he
was willing to serve in this church in this capacity. Due to some
theological concerns expressed by members of the body, Illiana was
seeking a respectful measure of accountability to a man ministering
within our borders while not belonging to our body. The option
C&C took was to proceed with the process of licensing him to
preach within our borders. Concurrence from the home presbytery
was not considered in the discussion and omitted.
Presbytery did not follow BCO 13-2, nor did
Presbytery seek concurrence from home presbytery of TE. BCO 132 states: “When a minister labors outside the geographical bounds
of, or in a work not under the jurisdiction of his Presbytery, at home
or abroad, it shall be only with the full concurrence of and under
circumstances agreeable to his Presbytery, and to the Presbytery
within whose geographical bounds he labors, if one exists.” No
reason given why TE’s home presbytery was not consulted for
concurrence.
To begin, this was an unorthodox situation. TE
[name omitted] was a TE in missouri Presbytery, neighboring Iliana
to the West, serving in a revitalization work when their former pastor
exited quickly. TE [name omitted] expressed to the congregation
that he did not intend to move his credentials to Illiana, however he
was willing to serve in this church in this capacity. Due to some
theological concerns expressed by members of the body, Illiana was
seeking a respectful measure of accountability to a man ministering
within our borders while not belonging to our body. The option C&C
took was to proceed with the process of licensing him to preach
within our borders. Concurrence from the home presbytery was not
considered in the discussion and omitted.
Presbytery did not follow BCO 13-2, nor did
Presbytery seek concurrence from home presbytery of TE. BCO 132 states: “When a minister labors outside the geographical bounds
of, or in a work not under the jurisdiction of his Presbytery, at home
or abroad, it shall be only with the full concurrence of and under
circumstances agreeable to his Presbytery, and to the Presbytery
within whose geographical bounds he labors, if one exists.” No
reason given why TE’s home presbytery was not consulted for
concurrence.
It was Iliana’s understanding that TE [name
omitted] had obtained concurrence of his presbytery at the time. This
was also Iliana’s understanding of the reading of ( BCO 13-2). The
presbytery now recognizes the error of this oversight of not
documenting it properly and will endeavor to document this more
diligently in the future. If the revitalization work had been successful
Illiana would have taken steps as soon as possible to correct this
inaction and followed BCO 13-2 direction. By God’s providence
this TE’s ministry in Illiana ended soon after these actions and this
particular work closed not long after this TE returned to his home
presbytery

description: Stated differences are not in candidate’s own words.
Stated differences not judged with prescribed categories
responses: Illiana recognizes the error and will strive to
accurately and fully document identified differences in the future.
The presbytery found that TE’s [name omitted] stated differences,
included here in his own words, were not exceptions as his view on
recreation on the Sabbath is commonly held, his view on creation
conforms to the analogical day position accepted by the PCA
General Assembly, and neither of these views were found to be
hostile to the system nor striking at the vitals of religion.
The only exception I take to the Standards is in WCF
21.8 regarding the prohibition on "recreations," also found
in WLC 117 and WSC 60 and 61. My rationale for this is
twofold.
First, to the best of my knowledge, the only proof text
that addresses this directly is Isa 58:13, which speaks of
"going your own ways, or seeking your own pleasure" on
the Sabbath. While at first blush this appears to prohibit
recreation, in my opinion, Isa 58:3 helps to interpret what
the prophet means by "pleasure": "Behold, in the day of
your fast you seek your own pleasure, and oppress all your
workers." In other words, "pleasure" here refers not to
common and lawful recreations, but injustice.
Second, the consistent and repeated Sabbath
prohibitions throughout Scripture are related to work and
commercial activity (e.g., Ex 20:10; Jer 17:19ff.; Neh 13).
Therefore, I believe this is the thrust of what we are resting
from, in order that we may worship the Triune God on the
Lord's Day. In short, so long as we prioritize public and
private worship, as well as acts of necessity and mercy, on
the Lord's Day -- which will necessarily consume the
majority of the day -- I do not believe it is sinful to engage
in limited amounts of non-commercial recreation.
I hold to an analogical view of the creation days. In
short, this means that I see the thrust of the creation account
being God's pattern-setting for His creation -- six days of
work, plus one day of rest (Ex 20:9-11). There is a certain
similarity between man being made in God's image, and
man's days being made in the likeness of God's days. And
just as God went out to work and then rest, so do we -- in a
similar way, but not in an identical way. Regarding the
length of the days, I am persuaded by the analogical view's
appreciation for their unspecified length. After all, the
Hebrew "yom" can take on many meanings and does not
force a 24-hour view. Likewise, other passages speak to the
mystery of how God relates to time (e.g., Job 10:5; Ps 90:4;
In terms of my understanding of the Confession's use
of the clause "in the space of six days," I see this primarily
as a reaction against an instantaneous creation view (e.g.,
Augustine). While the private writings of several divines
indicate they held to ordinary days, their omission of 24hour language calls into question whether they intended to
bind men on this point, especially within a consensus
document. Moreover, the fact that there is but one allusion
to the length of the days suggests that this topic was not of
first importance, as creation ex nihilo and Adam's federal
headship were.

description: Presbytery’s release of TE does not specify the judgment of the court as to the fidelity of the receiving body in maintaining the Word and Sacrament in their fundamental integrity.
responses: TE [name omitted] had taken a call to an
independent congregation. He was not present when this action was
taken. No note of input from either the Senior Pastor or the Session
is included, as the question was probably not asked.
There was no record of a determination whether
the new/independent church was faithful in maintaining the Word
and Sacraments in their fundamental integrity
Response: [2019] Illiana recognizes the necessity to release TEs
into works that will maintain the Word and Sacrament in their
fundamental integrity. At this particular meeting neither the Senior
Pastor at the time or this particular TE was present. The action was
brought by a member of the Session present and the question was
not asked. Since that time, the Senior Pastor at that time has been
contacted and he is aware of conversations with this particular
church and is willing to affirm that they do indeed maintain the
Word and Sacrament in their fundamental integrity and that this
particular TE was released to a new ministry that is faithful to the
scriptures.
The court has yet to specify their judgment as to
the fidelity of the receiving body.
f. As no response was received, a response must be submitted to
the following GA:

description: No record of review of session minutes.
Standing Rules provision redefines composition of Presbytery, by granting mission churches voting delegates to Presbytery.
No minutes from commission for Cono.
responses: Regarding the dates cited by RPR, in the following
places, the minutes of Iowa Presbytery meetings indicate that the
commission reported to the presbytery on issues with which they
were dealing and actions they had taken.
- March 11, 2017 - p.26 (lines 14-23) show the report of Cono
Commission to the presbytery.
- May 6, 2017 - p.26 (beginning at line 32) and p.46 (lines 1-21)
and Appendix B of those minutes show the report of Cono
Commission to the presbytery.
- July 8, 2017 - p 52 (lines 19-26) record the report of that
commission to the presbytery.
- November 11, 2017 - p.72 (lines 25 and following) and p.73
(lines 1-18) show the final report of the commission to the
presbytery
Because you cited those dates, we point you to those portions of
minutes of those meetings.
But we suspect that your concern is not that of the commission
reporting regularly to the presbytery but the presbytery reviewing its
minutes. The final review of the minutes of the meetings of that
commission was done when the Session of that congregation (the
commission) submitted their final minutes. They were reviewed by
the Ad.Nom. Committee and the committee’s motion to approve
those minutes was approved by Iowa Presbytery at its meeting on
We apologize for not recording such review of their records in
2017. Such review was planned to take place in the review of
Session records when the commission had completed its labors. This
was carried out by the Ad. Nom. Committee prior to and then
approved by Iowa Presbytery at is meeting on July 2, 2018.

description: Sermon from licentiates are required to be provided in both written and oral form.
responses: The James River Presbytery (JRP) acknowledges and
agrees with the Review of Presbytery Records Committee’s stated
exception and expresses its commitment to comply with all the
requirements for licensing examinations, including the presentation
of a written sermon, going forward .

description: No record of 2/3 required vote to pass amendment to Bylaws .
responses: The JRP acknowledges and agrees with the Review of
Presbytery Records Committee’s stated exception and expresses its
commitment to comply with the requirements for amending its
Bylaws. This exception results entirely from a failure by the
Assistant Recording Clerk to properly record the vote, not from a
failure to take a 2/3 vote. He will aim to correctly record these votes
in the future.

description: Failed to identify the exact section of Bylaws to be amended.
responses: The JRP acknowledges and agrees with the Review of

Bylaws. This exception results entirely from a failure by the
Assistant Recording Clerk to properly record the section of the
Bylaws being amended. The Stated Clerk’s Report to the Presbytery
clearly identified that section.

description: No mention of ⅔ vote required for suspension of Bylaws.
responses: The JRP acknowledges and agrees with the Review of
Presbytery Records Committee’s stated exception and expresses its
commitment to comply with the requirements for amending its
Bylaws. This exception results entirely from a failure by the
Assistant Recording Clerk to properly record the vote, not from a
failure to take a 2/3 vote. He will aim to correctly record these votes
in the future.

description: Church seemed to particularize and there is no record of a congregational vote petitioning presbytery, nor is there record of an election of a pastor and elders.
responses: The Korean Capital Presbytery does not
understand this exception of substance. The minutes says, “Change
of status of [church name omitted] Presbyterian Church from
mission church to particular church has been delegated to the
officers.” It does not state that the Presbytery approved the
particularization.
The record does not show that the steps required
by BCO 5-9 for particularization have been followed. If the officers
were constituted as a commission the record of their actions needs
to be included in the Presbytery’s minutes.

description: No record that calls to TEs were examined and approved by Presbytery ( BCO 20-1 ; 20-6 ; RAO 16-3.e.6).

Ordination of TEs: no record of call to a definite work. [name omitted]. Response [2018] : KCP agrees with this exception. All five candidates (four from April and one from October) received calls to a definite work, but the record only indicates the names of the churches from which the calls came from. We will include more detail next time. Rationale [2018] : Minutes need to reflect that the Presbytery acted to approve the calls of all five candidates ( BCO 20-1, 20-6, 20-8; RAO 16-3.e.6). Response [2019] : KCP agrees with the RPR’s decision. KCP reconsidered the matter and ratified the calls of all five candidates on 2018 October meeting.

Ordination examination approved conditionally. Response [2018] : KCP disagrees with this exception. BCO 21-4 does not forbid a conditional approval of an ordination examination. Concerning the candidate in question, we checked his qualifications and examined the candidate with the subject matter prescribed in BCO 21-4.c. He affirmed our confessional standards with any exception. KCP’s examination committee was satisfied with the result of his examination. However, during the floor test, a few presbytery members were concerned that the candidate’s explanations on some of the issues concerning sexuality and the ordination of women seemed lacking. He affirmed our position, but a few members felt that he needed extra study on the matter. KCP could have ignored the minority concern, but KCP usually tries to satisfy all of its members. Therefore, KCP decided to approve of the candidate with an instruction to write papers (2-3 pages each) concerning the two topics of sexuality and the ordination of women. KCP took a vote and it was unanimous.
responses: While the concept of admonishing or
encouraging an approved ordinand to further study is appropriate,
the language of “conditional approval” is not one acknowledged in
the BCO . It was likely that this was not, in fact, a conditional
approval, yet the response indicates it was, thus it being deemed
unacceptable.
Response [2019] : KCP agrees with the RPR’s decision. KCP
ratified the decision to approve his examination on October 2018
meeting.
f. That the following response to a previous assembly be found
unsatisfactory; therefore a new response must be submitted to
the following GA:

description: Minutes of commission not entered in Presbytery minutes (see also RAO 16-3.e.5). Response [2018] : KCP disagrees with this exception. We did not form any commissions or committees in 2016 except three committees for ordinations (one in April and two in October). There are reports from the committees, but there are no minutes. Rationale [2018] : The PCA does not have committees for ordination but commissions and commissions must submit their minutes to the Presbytery and must be entered into the Presbytery minutes.
responses: KCP agrees with the RPR’s decision. First, the
word was mistranslated. We have commissions for ordination, not
committee. KCP’s ordination commission only produces a report. A
chair of the commission, assigned by the moderator, arranges an
ordination service, and he notifies commissioners on time, place and
the order of service. Since there is no meeting a prior to the service,
there are no minutes. Due to the long distance between members,
having a prior meeting is difficult.
a service of ordination is apart of the meeting
and action of the commission and therefore requires a record ( BCO

responses: The candidate from KAPC was examined
according to the Standing Rules of KEP which states that TE
transferring from another denomination shall be examined on the
subjects form licensure or ordination at the discretion of the Candidate
Examination Committee.
The CEC decided to examine the candidates in the areas of BCO
and Church History only (omits Bible, Theology, Sacraments) since
the minister was transferring from KAPC which is very similar to
PCA (90%). KEP will be more careful in the future to record
accurately.
PCA rules state that ministers transferring from
non-PCA denominations shall be examined in all areas even if from
a denomination similar to the PCA. In addition, Presbytery bylaws
cannot be out of accord with the BCO ( BCO 13-6; 21-4).

responses: The mentioned TE had been already ordained at

Confession with Larger and Shorter Catechisms . He successfully
passed all the areas of the test and Presbytery did not find any
differences. Although the test result was written in general terms,

(b); 2. All specific requirements of ordination exam not recorded;

regret our failure to do it.

responses: Inexplicably, the minutes of the called meeting of

They are attached hereto with apology.

description: Use of extraordinary clause not explained. Actual 3/4 vote not recorded. Response : Upon further reflection, we believe we have erred in examining the man seeking to transfer into the PCA from the Presbyterian Church of Brazil, with whom the PCA has fraternal relations. We misinterpreted the language of BCO 13-6 to allow for us to use the extraordinary clause from BCO 21-4 to examine him according to views only, as if he were coming from another Presbytery. We did this because of his years of experience and training. The Presbytery vote was unanimous to use the extraordinary clause in this way (though not recorded in our minutes). However, in looking a tit more closely, the language of BCO 13-6 requires (shall) that a man coming from another denomination be examined in all areas according to BCO 21-4 (knowledge and views). We could have used the extraordinary clause at that time to omit parts of the examination based on his many years of experience in the Presbyterian Church of Brazil, several excellent references which were secured, and his work as the Director of MORE International. In that case, we would have clearly stated our reasons for doing so and recorded the ¾ vote of approval by the Presbytery. We acknowledge our error and will be careful in the future to adhere to the BCO 13-6 procedure for examination of those men coming from other denominations NOTE: the man in question is moving to North Carolina where he will continue as Director of MORE International and no doubt seek to be transferred to the Presbytery into whose bounds he moves

No approval by congregation of provisional session members. Response : New Jersey Presbytery responds that the session referred to is a commission of Presbytery. [Church name omitted] is a Mission Church of New Jersey Presbytery and is so listed in the roll included both with the report to RPR and in the instant minutes. Presbytery acted to fill vacancies and to replace some members of the existing Presbytery Commission serving as the governing body. There is no need for congregational approval in the case of a Mission Church. ( BCO 5-3 c.) Following is the citation from the minutes of November 18, 2017, indicating that the action was part of the report of the NJMNA Committee. [Church name omitted] reports to Presbytery through the NJ-MNA Committee: Minutes of November 18, 2017, Page 17 NJ-Mission to North America Committee TE [name omitted] reported for the committee, opening with prayer. TE [name omitted] apprised the committee of the need to further augment the session of [church name omitted] due to recent losses and changes in status. The committee: Moved: That Presbytery appoint the following elders to serve on the session for [church name omitted]: TE [name omitted], RE [name omitted], and RE [name omitted]. This carried. TE [ name omitted] presented a short report on the ministry of [church name omitted]. TE [name omitted] closed the report with prayer

Meeting (see Apr. 11, 2018 Minutes, 79-09).
Exception Jan 23, 2016 ( BCO 21-4) – Differences not stated in
candidate’s own words and not judged with prescribed categories.
Presbytery agrees with the committee’s noting this as an
exception but explains that the record did not accurately reflect the
fact that the candidate had no differences, stating in his own words
“I do not have exceptions to the Westminster Standards.” Presbytery
pledges to be more careful in the future and has corrected the record
to state that the candidate reported no differences with the Standards.

description: Installation commission report not included with Presbytery minutes.
responses: Presbytery acknowledges this omission and pledges to
approve and include future reports in its minutes but is unable to
correct the record since the commission report is not extant.

description: All specific requirements of licensure exam not reported.
responses: Presbytery agrees with the committee’s noting this as an
exception but explains that this paragraph, along with 71-26 and 7127, were erroneously included in the record and will be vacated. The
said candidate was in fact NOT licensed and his examination for
coming under care and approval of internship were previously
recorded, without exceptions, in paragraph 71-24.

description: Installation commission report not included with Presbytery minutes.
responses: Presbytery acknowledges this omission and pledges to
approve and include future reports in its minutes but is unable to
correct the record since the commission report is not extant.

description: No record of stated differences or how the Presbytery judged them.
responses: Presbytery agrees with this exception and pledges to
properly record candidate’s differences and Presbytery’s judgment
in the future. The candidate’s differences in his own words, which
were approved as more than semantic but not out of accord with any
fundamental of the system of doctrine, will be recorded in the
minutes and were as follows.
WCF- Ch 21.8. Recreation on the Sabbath: I hold to the
WCF view of the Sabbath as a whole, but the scriptural
proofs the confession uses aren’t precise when it comes
to recreation. I believe that Christ teaches plainly that
it is lawful to do good on the Sabbath (Mt. 12:12).
Primarily this is about works of necessity, but I believe
this can also apply to family time or other similar godly
endeavors such as recreational time with friends. I
don’t believe it is sinful to toss a ball in the back yard
with your family, or togo on a walk with your wife on
the Sabbath, or something similar. Loving people well
is a high calling, and is permitted to do on the Sabbath,
an dido not consider that to be a form of work.
However, I would not encourage families or persons to
abandon worship to attend sporting events, neglect
worship for travel sports, or anything similar. Isaiah
58:18 indicates “pleasure” as breaking of Sabbath
commands, but that pleasure in context regards
conducting business activities, not recreation.
LC 109. With respect to images, i do not believe it is a
violation of the 2nd Commandment to use images
depicting Jesus’ humanity. The Bible is clear in its
instruction not to create an idol to worship; I do not
believe that the text prohibits the use of images for
pedagogical uses. We should be careful that we
distinguish teaching versus worship. The two are not
equal. It is possible to use teaching aids, and they not
be worshiped in place of the Lord, one example being
teaching aids for children in Sunday School.
Additionally, I agree with David VanDrumnen’s
conclusion concerning the intent of the Westminster
divines that, “If indeed the Westminster divines were
of a mind to omit pictures of Christ’s humanity from
the proscriptions in Larger Catechism 109, they would
surely have needed to have stated this explicitly”
(David VanDrunen, “Pictures of Jesus and the
Sovereignty of Divine Revelation: Recent literature
and a Defense of the Confessional Reformed View,”
The Confessional Presbyterian 5 [2009] 214-227).
Because of these noted reasons, I believe that the use
of depictions of the humanity of Jesus for teaching
purposes is allowed. If these images ae used, then it
should be stated that the images are only for teaching
and not an accurate portrayal of Jesus for worship.

description: No Ruling Elders appointed to commission for ordination.
responses: Presbytery agrees with this omission, acknowledging our
error in not recording the names of the Ruling Elders that were
appointed, and promises to accurately record appointees in the
future. The record will be updated to reflect that REs [names
omitted] were appointed to the Commission.

description: No record of stated differences or how the Presbytery judged them.
responses: Presbytery agrees with this exception and pledges to
properly record candidate’s differences and Presbytery’s judgment
in the future. The candidate’s differences in his own words, which
were approved as more than semantic but not out of accord with any
fundamental of the system of doctrine, will be recorded in the
minutes and were as follows:
WCF 21-8: This section, along with WSC 60 and
WLC 117, forbids acts of recreation on the Sabbath
day. i take exception to this statement because i believe
it is contrary to what the Bible teaches. Jesus says that
the Sabbath was made for man, not man for the
Sabbath. Ex. 23:12 states that the Sabbath is both for
rest and refreshment – spiritually and physically.
Finally, the WLC 121 helps us understand that one way
to practice “remembrance” on the Sabbath is through
thankful remembrance of creation. Thus, since the
Sabbath was made for humans, in part for their physical
refreshment, and for thankful remembrance of creation,
I believe non-organized recreation, such as hiking or
biking, is a practical way to observe the Sabbath. This
is because recreation can be very refreshing for most
humans and is a way to enjoy God’s creation.
WLC 109 : Since Christ is the “image of the invisible
God” (Col 1:15), and the “exact imprint of his nature”
(Heb 1:3), one cannot help but picture Christ inwardly
when reading the Gospels or taking the Lord’s supper.
Thus, I take exception to the statement that we cannot
make any representations of God inwardly, as the
Confession states. Furthermore, while I affirm that we
cannot make any representations of God for the
purpose of worship, I believe we can use images of the
humanity of Christ for educational purposes – but not
for worship.
WLC 151 : Though on one level all sins are equally
worthy of God’s wrath and require Christ’s atoning
blood, I affirm WLC 151 when it says that some sins
bring us more guilt and do us more damage. Thus, I
affirm most of WLC 151, such as when it says sins
against a weaker brother are greater (Rom 14:13);
however, there is an aspect of WLC that I disagree
with. I take exception to the assertion that the day in
which sin is committed (i.e., the Lord’s Day) makes it
worse.
WLC 191: While I affirm that Christians should submit
themselves to governing authorities (Rom 13:1), I do
not believe this implies that the authority of the church
is under the state. I therefore take exception to the idea
that the church is “countenanced and maintained by the
civil magistrate”; I believe that Christ alone is the one
who supports and maintains the church (Eph. 1:22).

the areas of Christian Experience, Theology, Sacraments, Principles
and Rules of Government and Discipline of PCA and that all
examinations for transfer of ordination were approved. No declared
differences or changes in previous view of Confession or
Catechisms. Minutes state that Mr. [name omitted] was examined in
the areas of Sacraments, Church History, PCA History and Rules of
Discipline and that all examinations for ordination were approved.
No record of BCO 21-4-c.1.c for Bible Content and
Theology for individual examined for ordination.
Mr. [name omitted] was examined in the areas of
Bible Content and Theology and these examinations were approved.

description: Candidate examination areas are not indicated – generic “all areas” not sufficient.
No record of congregational or sessional vote concerning dissolution of pastoral relationships.
Consideration of complaint was deferred until after the next Stated Meeting.
Presbytery has not reviewed Session records for 4 years.
responses: Presbytery has not reviewed Session records for 4 years.
Our inaction on Session Records needs action . “ As directed by the
2018 General Assembly, the Philadelphia Presbytery has begun to
work to read and approve Session Minutes, beginning with 2018 and
then reviewing previous years. The collection of minutes began in
2018 and the review of minutes has continued through 2019.
Churches will be cited if they fail to submit their minutes to
Presbytery as directed by the provisions of the BCO .”

description: TE laboring out of bounds without concurrence Presbytery within whose bounds he labors.
No record of declaration of quorum.
responses: Presbytery agrees with the exception and will plan to
record said declarations going forward. Presbytery has reviewed its
records and confirmed that a quorum was indeed present for each
meeting in question.

description: All requirements of ordination exam not recorded; all listed items need to be approved.
Stated differences not recorded in candidate’s own words.
Stated differences not recorded in candidate’s own words.
Use of extraordinary clause not explained.
TE’s change in call not recorded. No record of congregational meeting for associate pastor or presbytery approval of call.
Stated differences not recorded in candidate’s own words.
No attachments were included even though referenced throughout.
Minutes from executive session not included.
responses: We sincerely apologize, the lack of executive session
minutes as part of the Feb 14, 2017 Presbytery minutes are without
excuse. The contents of the executive session consisted of a TE
taking a leave of absence as an announcement. This announcement
had been released to the congregation two days earlier and thus we
made the announcement in Executive Session in error. Although this
had been corrected in the minutes we neglected to reflect the change.
A criteria for entering Executive Session has been implemented to
ensure that this oversight does not occur in the future.

description: Minutes from executive session not included.
responses: We sincerely apologize for the oversight in the original
submission. The Presbytery conducted a transition of Stated Clerks
and a transition to anew file storage and distribution platform in
2017-2018. Due to an unknown error, three records were completely
corrupted in their original format. We are working to reconstruct and
reapprove the records, which includes the aforementioned Executive
Session minutes. The substance of the Executive Session minutes
were two actions: 1) the receipt of request for reference, and 2) the
establishment of a commission to request for reference. The actions
of this commission were captured in resolved in later executive
session minutes of Presbytery.

description: All specific requirements of ordination exam not included.
responses: TE [name omitted]’s ordination exam was split between
the May, August and November Presbytery meetings. The August
meeting included Christian Experience and Calling (21-4.c.1.a),
English Bible (21-4.c.1.c), Theology (21-4.c.1.d), Church
Government (21-4.c.1.h) and Representative Sermon (21-4.c.4).
The November meeting included Church History (21-4.c.1.f),
Sacraments (21-4.c.1.e), Exegetical Paper (21-4.c.3) and
Theological Paper (21-4.c.2). We neglected to include an acceptance
of his Seminary Degree in lieu of an oral examination of the original
languages (21-4.c.1.b) in accordance with 21.c.1. We sincerely
apologize for the oversight and we will be sure to explicitly call out
this as part of a checklist in future meetings.

description: no record of TE transfer being examined on Christian experience and views as explained in BCO 13-6.
No record of release from presbytery in TE transfer.
Failure to record the specific areas of the floor examination of [names omitted].
Installation commission did not have at least 2 REs.
No record of congregational concurrence in dissolution of pastoral relations.
Records of commission not entered in Presbytery minutes (cf. RAO 16-3.e.4).
Overture not recorded.
Minutes of executive session not included.
No record of call to definite work.
No record of approval of call by presbytery.
responses: The Presbytery acknowledges its failure to record the
approval of the calls by the Presbytery. However, the calls of both
men were approved at the 109 [th] stated meeting (January 27-28,
2017) and the minutes of that stated meeting have been amended to
reflect the approval.

description: Minutes of executive session not included.
responses: The Presbytery entered into Executive Session to hear a
pair of written communications regarding a sensitive pastoral
situation. The communications were read and the only action taken
during the Executive Session was to rise out of executive session.
The main minutes previously submitted for review recorded that in
Executive Session the two written communications were read, and
that thereafter it was moved, seconded, and approved to exit
executive Session. In the future the Presbytery will endeavor to
make it clear that no additional executive minutes were taken or
necessary in light of what has been recorded in the main minutes.

description: No record of congregational/ session meeting in dissolution of pastoral/assistant pastor relationship.
no record in minutes of 2017 exceptions taken by GA and/or Presbytery approval of responses to the exceptions.
Stated differences not judged.
Minutes of commission not entered in Presbytery minutes (see RAO 16-3.e.4).
Differences of three candidates not judged to prescribed categories.
Differences not stated in candidates’ own words.
Minutes say no quorum present, and no record of attendance attached.
Ordination commission reported but no records of proceedings attached.
TEs laboring out of bounds or without call reports missing.
A South Florida TE laboring out of bounds without concurrence of Presbytery within whose bounds he labors.
¾ vote for waiving internship requirement not recorded.
No record of review of records of church sessions.
responses: That’s because there were none performed. We agree
with the exception. Session records were reviewed at the February
14, 2017 Stated Presbytery Meeting for the years 2015 and 2016.
Directed Stated Clerk to have Session Minutes reviewed each year

description: Stated differences not judged.
responses: We agree with this exception; future minutes will
properly reflect the decision of Presbytery .

description: – Stated difference not recorded in the minister’s/candidate’s own words ( RAO 16-3.e5).
responses: Actually, it was. If the candidate says he is taking the
“Continental Exception to the Sabbath per 21-8” then that is what
he said in his own words
Rationale : Candidate must state “specific instances in which he”
( BCO 19-2.e) differs from WCF, LC, SC; not simply name a view.

description: All specific requirements of ordination exam not recorded (No record of exegetical or theological papers. Unclear if ordinand had been previously licensed).
Candidate does not state in his stated differences from what particular part of our standards he differs.
Stated differences not judged with prescribed categories, and not recorded in candidates’ own words.
Call for meeting not in order or recorded.
Stated differences in candidates own words referenced but left out of minutes.
Presbytery rules as written appear to forbid local churches from issuing calls prior to consulting the presbytery shepherding committee.
¾ vote for waiving internship requirements not recorded.
No reason given for a man ordained a san assistant pastor to be commissioned a san evangelist.
Minutes do not list all specific requirements for ordination
The report of a judicial commission was not included in the appendix as noted.
– Stated differences not recorded in the minister’s own words .
responses: The failure to include the differences as part of the
appendices of the minutes of June 2, 2016 and October 6, 2016 were
an oversight on the part of the Clerk. He has made changes to his
storage and minutes preparation to prevent this from happening in
the future
Stated differences not submitted.

description: Stated differences not attached as noted [name omitted].
responses: The failure to include the differences as part of the
appendices of the minutes of June 2, 2016 and October 6, 2016 were
an oversight on the part of the Clerk. He has made changes to his
storage and minutes preparation to prevent this from happening in
the future
Stated differences not submitted.

description: No record of congregational meeting to dissolve the call of a pastor.
Shepherding Committee acted as a commission without authorization.
Minutes of commission not entered into Presbytery minutes [name omitted].
responses: “Presbytery agrees with the exception. The minutes
failed to include the records of the commission to install the TE as
required by BCO 15-12. That report is included. Presbytery will be
more careful in the future.”

description: Candidate states one difference, but Presbytery grants two exceptions. So the second difference is not stated in his own words at the meeting. An editing error resulted in the second exception being listed when that candidate only and one exception. Presbytery will be more careful in the future.”
No record of call.
Records of commission not entered into presbytery minutes.
No record of call.
All specific ordination requirements not recorded (theology exam missing).
responses: Wisconsin Presbytery acknowledges that the minutes of
January 23, 2016 do not show a theology examination for Mr. [name
omitted], who presbytery examined for ordination at that meeting .
Wisconsin Presbytery further acknowledges it was negligent in not
including a note explaining that presbytery gave Mr. [name omitted],
a very thorough and rigorous examination in theology at the time of
his Licensure examination on September 26, 2015 and did not think
another theology exam was necessary so soon after the September 26,
2015 exam. Presbytery also recognizes it should have included
another theology exam at the time of Mr. [name omitted]’s
ordination examination so the minutes would reflect that presbytery
met the requirements of B CO 21-4.
Editorial Note: As of the date of this report, there are 88 presbyteries.
Southwest Presbytery (Presbytery #79 above) was divided into Arizona and
Rio Grande Presbyteries as of December 31, 2017.
